Home / Hourly vs Fixed Rate Calculator

Hourly vs Fixed Rate Calculator

Calculate the hourly rate, monthly retainer, and project equivalents needed to reach your annual income goal.

Scenarios:

How to Use This Tool

1
Enter your target annual income goal.
2
Set your realistic billable hours per week (most freelancers bill 20-30).
3
Enter how many weeks you plan to take off per year.
4
Review your required hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly retainer rates.
5
Check the assessment to see if your target rate is realistic for your market.
6
Scroll down to compare what different income goals require from your pricing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many billable hours per week is realistic?

Most full-time freelancers bill 20-30 hours per week. The remaining time goes to admin, marketing, proposals, learning, and breaks. If you are billing 40 hours every week, you are likely burning out. Plan for 25 billable hours as a sustainable target.

Should I charge hourly or fixed-price?

Fixed-price is generally better for experienced freelancers. Clients prefer predictable costs, and you earn more when you work efficiently. Use hourly for unclear scopes or ongoing retainer work. Start with hourly, then transition to fixed as you learn to estimate accurately.

What if my target hourly rate is too high?

You have three levers: increase billable hours, lower your income goal, or specialize in a higher-paying niche. Working with US/EU clients while living in a lower-cost region is a proven strategy to make a high effective rate achievable.

Should I charge different rates to different clients?

Yes. Early clients might get a lower rate for testimonials. Enterprise clients should pay premium rates. Long-term retainer clients can receive a 10-20% discount. The key is to never disclose your rate variance — each client gets a custom quote based on project scope.

How do I factor in taxes and business expenses?

Multiply your desired take-home income by 1.3-1.5 to cover self-employment taxes (15.3% in the US), health insurance, software, hardware, and professional development. If you want $100K take-home, target $130K-$150K in gross revenue.

Results

Copy All
Example

💼 Hourly vs Fixed Pricing Model

💰 Required Rate Snapshot: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • Annual Income Goal: $100,000 • Annual Expenses: $5,000 • Net Target (income+exp): $105,000 • Billable Hours/Week: 30 hrs • Weeks Off Per Year: 4 wks • Working Weeks: 48 wks • Annual Billable Hours: 1,440 hrs 📐 Key Metrics: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • Required Hourly Rate: $72.92/hr (after expenses) • Daily Rate (8 hrs): $583/day • Weekly Rate: $2,188/wk • Monthly Retainer: $8,750/mo • Fixed Project (4-week): $8,750/project • Utilization Rate: 75% of 40-hr work week 🩺 Rate Health: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • 🟡 Rate $72.92/hr is mid-market — requires solid expertise and client trust. Specialize deeply to justify this rate. • 🟢 30 billable hrs/wk is full-time sustainable. • 🟢 Utilization at 75% — sustainable without burnout. • 🟢 Expenses are 5% of income target — lean operation. 🎯 Income Ladder: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • $30,000/yr → $24.31/hr | $2,917/mo retainer • $60,000/yr → $45.14/hr | $5,417/mo retainer • $100,000/yr → $72.92/hr | $8,750/mo retainer • $150,000/yr → $107.64/hr | $12,917/mo retainer • $200,000/yr → $142.36/hr | $17,083/mo retainer • $300,000/yr → $211.81/hr | $25,417/mo retainer ⚖️ Hourly vs Fixed Project Break-Even: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • Hourly model, 4 weeks: $8,750 for 120 hrs • Fixed model, 4 weeks: $8,750 for 120 hrs (assumes same effort) • 🟡 Hourly and fixed-fee are roughly equivalent at this rate. Pick based on client preference and scope clarity. • Break-even fixed project rate: $72.92/hr (same hourly, all-in price). 🔄 What-If Scenarios: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • Raise rate 20%: New rate $87.50/hr (need only 1200 billable hrs/yr) • Cut expenses 20%: New rate $58.33/hr (saves $$1,000/yr) • Add 5 billable hrs/wk: New rate $62.50/hr (more income, same goal) • Cut 2 weeks off: New rate $76.09/hr (compress to 46 working wks) • 2 income sources: Split goal in half, lower hourly pressure on each. 💡 Top Lever: Standard tier — your differentiator is reliability and process. Document your workflow and offer guarantees to command higher rates. $30,000/yr → $24.31/hr | $2,917/mo retainer | $194/day $60,000/yr → $45.14/hr | $5,417/mo retainer | $361/day $100,000/yr → $72.92/hr | $8,750/mo retainer | $583/day $150,000/yr → $107.64/hr | $12,917/mo retainer | $861/day $200,000/yr → $142.36/hr | $17,083/mo retainer | $1,139/day $300,000/yr → $211.81/hr | $25,417/mo retainer | $1,694/day
